Thank you. I have been delegated authority as Jason a responsible committee member is in transit
he's on a flight right now. So kia ora tatou. My name is Julie Shepard. I am the senior environmental planner for
Piritako Tribal Authority and I am also the writer of the submissions. So if we can kick into that
Piritako Tribal Authority opposes the Māori purposes bill as currently proposed while we acknowledge
the intent to modernize and streamline processes for Māori landowners. We believe the bill lacks
sufficient safeguards to protect Māori landowner rights aligned with the principles of Te Tiriti
o Waitangi and ensure the long-term retention of whenua Māori. The Māori Affairs Committee holds
a critical responsibility to safeguard the interests of whenua Māori. While you have our written submission
I must stress the significant risks posed by the proposed amendments to the Māori Trust Act
1953 which forms part of the Māori purposes bill. Our key concerns with the Māori Trust Act
amendment is clause 71 removing the judicial oversight and land transfers which heightens
the risk of alienation and undermines the collective decision-making. Clause 85, the simplified
land acquisition processes fail to address affordability and cultural appropriateness.
Disadvantaged Māori who already face significant resource limitations. Clause 88 aligning with
general land transfer laws erodes Te Kanga Māori and introduces unnecessary legal complexities.
19 replacing the Minister of
Māori affairs with a general minister diminishes Māori-specific advocacy.
Course 95, regulatory updates, risk-creating inconsistencies and administrative burdens
for under-resourced Māori entities. Together, these changes pose a direct threat
to Tēnōranga, Tēnātanga and the long-term retention of whenua Māori. Of course, this bill
has broader implications. These risks are compounded by proposed changes to the whenua Māori Act,
1993, developed by Te Puni Kōkiri under the direction of Minister Pōtaka.
These changes further undermine iwi Māori land rights specific concerns include
central register of owners' trustees, centralising sensitive information,
lease terms, extending lease terms to 99 years without court oversight,
locks future generations out of decision-making and access to the whenua. Crown-imposed
governance, temporary crown-imposed governance on ungoverned land bypasses collective decision-making
in te kāngamārī, enabling external control and eroding Tēnōranga-tērātanga. It becomes
quite overwhelming, I have to say. Whenua Mārī, we all understand, as a tāngatukuiho, integral
to Māori identity and well-being, the history of systemic land loss from the New Zealand land
wars to the native land court demands vigilance to prevent further alienation. The upcoming
referendum framework bill further illustrates these dangers with its silence on Te Tiriti o Waitangi
the potential for misinformation campaigns, threatening to bury indigenous rights and
silence iwi Māori voices. We only need to look back to understand the consequences of
further steps in stolen land. This is further exacerbated by the ongoing construction of
prisons which disproportionately target and incarcerate Māori perpetuating colonial harm
and systemic oppression. History has shown us that injustices against iwi Māori
remain entrenched in a relentless cycle of repetition rendering crown
apologies hollow and devoid of meaning words without transformative action no
longer hold significance. When these policies and actions unfold they
represent a deliberate continuation of colonial strategies perpetuating the
systematic erosion of Māori rights under te te re te o waitangi. This cannot and
must not be accepted. The Māori Affairs Committee has a duty to intervene and
halt this destructive trajectory. Failure to act will have devastating
consequences and history will not absolve those who stood by and enabled
this harm. Pirarako Tribal Authority urges the committee to reject the Māori
Purposes Bill in its current form. We call for meaningful engagement with iwi
Māori to ensure that any legislative changes uphold the principles of te
te re te o waitangi, protect te no rangatiratanga and safeguard the legacy of
iwi Māori for future generations. Ngā mihi nui kia koutou katoa. Thank you for listening tomorrow.
Thank you and I'll leave this just a just a couple of minutes for a couple of
questions if there are any from members any questions from members at this time.
just thank you for the thorough submission. Yeah it was a very thorough submission
Julie so thank you very much for the time that you put into it and we

and so forth. I think this I acknowledge that making it easier for the
administration of Mario's organisations I've no problem with that but I think
the intent is 180 years too late. I don't think legislation could put what
Mario's purpose are. I think Mario has been telling environmentally what their
purpose is and telling the Crown for 180 years that you've ignored so I do not
think in any way that this legislation can be put together as Mario's
purpose bill and so forth. We want to hear from Mario what their purpose is. We
don't want to hear what the Crown and the Crown's representation
that have a contract just to safeguard the Crown's assets and so forth to put it forward.
We want to hear from Mary and we want to hear from the Marry Queen, what the vision and the
vision of what going forward we have. So yeah, that's where I stand on this matter.
Okay, thank you very much Craig. Any questions from members?
Hey Craig, just want to say that you've always been very consistent with your position.
Really a comment and that's around, I don't know if you've had the opportunity to read any of the
annual reports of Iwida, most of them are published online. You might be interested in some of the
visions that are articulated within there. And of course, it's not to say that all Māori think the
same way and have the same vision, it's not the correct actually. But yeah, I just thought
I'd mention it, see if you might be interested in having a look at them.
I think my point though, Adrian, is that how it's portrayed to the park here and the white
person, it's probably always done in Māori language and so forth. I attended when the Māori party ended
Parliament to the lower Marae there and with two hours of Māori, which referred to me like going to
a church in Ireland, I had two hours of all that in mass, you know,
uncomprehensible. So I think it's got to be put out in English and a very simple sort of what the
future holds and what Māori would like instead of the other way.
around I was told like my first thought of this legislation was it's just dog
legislation it's just a crown trying to put Māori into how they want to to treat them.
Okay thank you. Okay I thank you very much Craig.
Kia ora, thank you.

Okay well so this is the first time I've done this and I'm really
I think I think a lot of people are really tired of what government's doing about
us Māoris as people of the land and I'm one of those people that grew up in the generation where
I was a half tongue and half Māori but they didn't consider me as a Māori to be in a Māori class
and I've been through a lot of rough times with the society trying to find myself as a Māori
like this society actually took me away from my culture and I had to take time out
to find my way back all by myself you know with all of these I'm really proud that there's Māori
out there putting out like stuff for us to learn from and I appreciate that so much because if it
wasn't for those people I would have lost my culture so I mean I'm all for the Māoris
like big time man I'm Māori like from blood from birth and all these legislations that China's
us. It's unacceptable. It's unfair for our people. I mean, if you guys are really looking to, I mean,

Why are they still trying to take stuff away from us? And yeah, man, that's the first time.
So I don't know if you guys got any questions I'll answer as best as I can.
Okay. Well, John, first of all, thank you very much for partaking in the process that we
that's important that we do have engagement from people like yourself and many others
that makes the system stronger and it's important that you have a voice here.
So I appreciate that. So any questions from members here?
Not really a question. Thank you, John. We don't get enough people submitting. So

and I don't want to force you into actually talking about that. But

My name is Maxine Williams.

So my main thing about this
is the change of the word Māori.
So 1867, the term Māori in this particular act
means a male Aboriginal native inhabitant of New Zealand
of the age of 21 years in upwards
and shall include half-castes.
Then 1902, the word Māori means an Aboriginal inhabitant
of New Zealand and includes half-castes
and their descendants by natives.
The same thing meant the same thing in 1905
and the electoral 1905 act.
Again, the same meaning was applied in 1927 act.
Then we get to the 1956 electoral act
and Māori means a person belonging
to the Aboriginal race of New Zealand
and includes a half-caste and a person intermediate
in blood between half-castes
and persons of pure descent from that race.
And then we come to 1991.
Māori means a person of the Māori race of New Zealand.
and includes a descendant of any such person and that was the Ministry of
Māori Development Act. Māori means a person of the Māori race of New Zealand
and includes any descendant of such a person, 1993 electoral act. My whole
point about putting in the submission is that that word hasn't
been changed since 1927. It went to 1956 and there was a change and then a change
again in 1991, 1993 and we're changing it again. Granted that you're you're only
adding the Macron across the top of the letter A but it appears given what's
happening with how people are identifying now. Now my father was born in 1922.
Ngāpuhi, Whakapapa's down one line from Hungi Hika and if we go up to Hungi
Hika and we go across to Hungi Hika's brother he comes down the other line as
well. So 1922 he served in the Māori Battalion. He would be 103 today.
We grew up, I didn't realize what I grew up with and I didn't value that until the
last 10 years when I'm watching what I knew as a Māori to be is more is
morphing into encompassing you know others and stuff and changing and I
really it really bothers me that this is the case it's I mean you've got overseas
you've got overseas people wanting to to claim being
Māori. Why? And with this little change of the macron above the A, it may seem so simple,
but where does that place me? And then all my 2.0 before me now, are we brought across
to be encompassed in a whole other type of what Māori means? I don't know. It just really
bothers me. This whole Māori Purpose Bill, I mean, I've had no tertiary education. I left
school at the end of third form. Anything I do know is because I've read and I believe
that my generation had one of the best years of education ever in this country. I grew up
in South Auckland. At that time, everyone around me, all the Māoris in our area in Manurua, we
all had mortgages on our homes. My parents had a mortgage. All of us at school had mortgages.
We all had contact back to our marae because my father was the oldest of the oldest. We were
always back to Whangarua for tummy. And now, you know, what I'm hearing and what of the lack of
or how we were displaced, it's just, it's not true. And it wasn't true for me. And maybe
my father being the oldest may have, have given us a bit of privilege. But I don't think so
because all the Māori kids I went to school with, we all went back to our marae. We all knew
where we came from. And suddenly it doesn't appear that suddenly I'm hearing that people
were misplaced. And I was a correction officer at one stage. Many Māori in prison don't know
their whakapapa.
happened to know because of my father's surnames and areas and I had a lot of
conversations with our Māori prisoners and I could tell them it's like you
start here go looking there I could take some of their whakapapa and some of
their names and help them find that and I can still do that today so this whole
change as small as it may be is I just feel it's a bit of a threat to
where I stand as Māori today so I'm sorry they have to interrupt you we've got a

time in submitting so well done for that and it's great to see new people being

here we are truly trying to find ways to have better outcomes for Māori around
the place that's our endeavor itself and the submissions like your just that
reminds us of the many challenges we still have before us itself
unfortunately we don't have any time for questions but Maxine thank you very

It was a recommendation of the review done in 2022

that the status of the Moriori language
be recognized by the use of that word

Now, so much of what is proposed

It seemed to me that it was unusual
that the bill should have come forward
without that change being made
and without any explanation.
So on the basis of my respect for the people
who did the review in 2000.
in 2022, I thought I would submit and say, well, it should go back in. The crown has for a long time
now recognized the particular situation of te re moriori and the fact that it is to all intents and
purposes as a spoken language largely extinct. But I'm a tremendous admirer of the efforts being made
by te re moriori to bring the language back again. And the crown, both parties, leading parties,
have recognized that and supported it. And it seemed to me that this was a minor but significant
step. And I just took the authority of the review committee on whose advice most of the changes
have been put forward to say, well, it's good enough for them. It certainly good enough for me.
So also because of the care that you've taken in terms of your thoughts,
one of the things that this committee is looking at is the wider ecosystem for
Te Whakapiawai or Te Reo Maori. And you look at both of them here. So,
Tomata Wai and Aroha Mai, is the one that you look at. Could you speak a bit more to your
understanding of the role of Tomata Wai and what role you'd like to see it playing in that ecosystem?
Sir, this is with reference to your careful observations in point three.
I think that the Temapa Wai is the leader of revitalisation among iwi and Māori, as it
says in the act, and of course most particularly the leader of revitalisation among hapū does
an awful lot at that level and in urban communities as well, and I think that
Temapa Wai should be respected for the work that it has done, and I think that
it's also been responsible for inspiring at the macro level, at the
national level, inspiring a focus on language planning, which has been so
important as the whole basis of revitalisation. Having said that, the point
of my submission here, in terms of the spirit of service to the public, the
proposal, which is not in line with the suggestions of the 2022 review, the
proposal to remove the spirit of service to the public as one of Temapa Wai's
aims, seems to me to undermine Temapa Wai's contribution to the whole nation.
Revitalisation of the Māori language is not of benefit only to iwi and to Māori,
they are the owners of the Māori language, there's no doubt about that, nonetheless
the existence of the Māori language is, as the act says, of value to the nation,
and I felt that the removal of the idea of spirit of service to the public,
making Temapa Wai the only organisation that that had ever been actively
removed from, put a preposition in the wrong place,
to do that sent an unusual message suggesting that somehow revitalisation was not also for the public because to my mind it is.
Unfortunately we're out of time but Colin before you go thank you very much for your very thoughtful submission and very well researched submission
and it's something for us to think about what you've contributed today so thank you very much Colin.
Okay, thank you.

Greetings, members of the committee.
Thank you for having me here today to present this opportunity to speak for you.
No ye men by all precinct, I stand before you as a woman,
living fresh flesh and breath.
Not a corporate fiction, not a statutory abstraction,
but as the private agent and authorised representative of the trust and
estate known in commerce as Mary and many of them limited.
I speak today under divine law, natural law of a law of nation and universal law
with full consciousness of the political and spiritual standing as a national.
the landian and ascendant of the AB originals, the native land-grantors. The co-heirs and living
moody whose names and inheritance are not inscribed, not on your roles but in the report of heaven and
earth. I do hereby give public notice. All assumed powers of attorney are revoked. Do not consent to
contract register perform under your legislative bill without full disclosure, mutual agreement and
honorable terms. This moody purpose of omnibus bill poses alterations to language, land and
office that trespass upon sacred agreement. You seek to redefine moody through your own construct
placing a macron where no consent was given, thereby displacing the original role of the
native trustee and violating the naming rights of lineage. The legal substitution of gendered
turn, his, her, or the neutered, there, is a deliberate attack on natural succession and
lawful inheritance. This reconfiguration is not linguistic convenience that is legislative warfare.
You attempt to reclassify new monster as te waipounamun without the consent of the
original grantors of their living agents. By what lawful authority do you pursue to rename
what you did not create? Let it be known, I stand as heir and co-heir and successor
to the principal, Samuel Brown, Charlie Brown and Robert Brown who retain all rights, interest
and equitable title to native land grants predating 1852. I do not submit those interests to the
oversight of your statutory moody trustee nor do I accept that the trust is dissolved,
This bill cloaked in reform is in fact a seizure, a subtle removal of ancient signposts and
lines of authority.
What is your comprehension regarding the use of reo Māori, not te reo Māori?
What authorises you to alter the spiritual tongue of a people and to mummetise and understatute?
The reo Māori belongs not to the crown, not to departments but to the breath and record
of ancestral lines.
To this committee, I say plainly, we may have jurisdiction over legal persons but not over
the living.
I appear in peace and in good faith and with clean hands.
Let this record stand, monk protunk, reserved in perpetuity.
I am of the family brown, private agent of the principal, co-aware, lawful successor and
authorised voice of living, estate and trust, your most obedient servant.
Alongside this, and in conjunction with this, I remain in honour without vexation, without
waiver, without acquiescence, that justice, not legislation, be done.
Kia ora.
Kia ora, Mary.

New Munster, the South Island, as the AB, original German inhabitants named it, cannot

The people in the area of Polydorf, Ranzau, Motere and Machuaika retain their

The attempt by this committee to use

was clear in Fixed General 1st Muldoon, that the pretended power of suspending of laws or
the execution of laws by regal authority without consent of Parliament is illegal.
Objection. I do pray that the removal of the words in Wellington and nature of the
business requiring board members to be present at such location of the earth at
the time may affect the requirements of the tender of the Crown assembled at
Westminster. New Munster is directly bound to Port Nicholson by deed and

So such action is a forfeiture of my person right to know the area of land
that the board or chairman are performing the nature of the business
and what the nature of the business is. The last section finally the Marryland
in the South is currently part of a dispute and claim of interest by myself
Parkha Aboriginal Inhabitant Catherine Sixtus and Mary Amelia Brown at the
Waitangi Tribunal concerning international agreements between the
Government of the German Reich and the Government of New Zealand regarding the
release of property rights and interests of German nationals. Namely Article 1
and Article 2 that the New Zealand Government and German Reich agreed to
release and transfer property and chiddles to the original German owners and
persons deriving titles through them. All Marry entities must give
affect
to their obligations to such international covenants, which include tangible and intangible
hair red detamines signed by native chiefs during the years of 1838 Provisional Bell,
1841 John Walt Ward, 1920 and 1929 of the German Reich. Such properties currently
in the process of claim at the Waitangi Tribunal is respective of all land and chattels
pertaining to New Munster, Paulie Dorf, Ranzel and the Wairau Rua Pukie, Akaroa and
Chinam island. A repeal of section 101d powers of the board with respect to land and chattels
may be a forfeiture of my person and an interference in ancient international treatises
annexed to my person. The committee can just bring your can you bring your submission to a
close place because we're just going. The committee has failed to consider Treaty of Waitangi North
and New Munster Treaty called Tettoriti and has failed to consider international obligations.
Therefore, until the committee corrects its errors, the bill ought not pass. I am Catherine Sixters
of the Family Sixters and revoke all your powers to pass this bill until such
